full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Web 2.0.

This was like the late December, early January. And within the first 24 hours it was super clear to me that this was different and the future in a way that I hadn't really experienced since maybe the early 2000s. Like I got genuinely excited about building things with this. And it was, I think I had sent a tweet at the time, I was like, 'this is the first time I felt like I've been living in the future since ChatGPT.'

And I, you know, in 15 minutes I built a new web interface uh to update my photo frames and they, you know, it built me this beautiful interface. I updated the frames, I got new photos on them, super happy.

Because that would be like I don't know you're in some incredible fine dining restaurant and they just delight you you know in the 11 Madison Park you know unreasonable hospitality way. I'll call this like the unreasonable open claw hospitality where it said to me when I was doing some work on like a bunch of names of people in Japan that I wanted to uh do like a little uh networking with. full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Greg Kara

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Well, I think the—the other thing that I really fell in love with immediately was the... there were sort of two things. Well, three. The—the memory feature, right? Like all of us using, uh, AI have used ChatGPT and the big models, right? And it has like some memory of you and you can kind of go check it out and there's probably—there's some other, like, voodoo magic they're doing in the background to, like, make it so you can access your former chats but it's like—it's like loose and you—you never really know whether it's going to work. And so the idea that like I'm getting this—these memory files on my own computer that I can then use and reuse and do things with and there's like basically a journal every day, like that was number one. Number two, the skills feature, uh, which, you know, you've seen skills like you said Jason, there's skills on Claude Code, like skills is like this new emerging primitive that's happening. But the idea that you could tell the Claude bot or the now Open Claude to build itself a skill and then simply share it with the community using ClaudeHub and then other people immediately start downloading it. I did this with a snow report skill, uh, right out of the gate of course, uh, and built some other ones but this really easy ability to build your own skills and then share them with the community I think is an underrated feature. Uh, and then the third thing is this like crazy idea that, uh, Peter had which is called Heartbeat. Which is he gave the bot a heartbeat. And you know, we've all built—anybody who's built software builds these cron jobs and you get the—there's these things called demons and cron jobs and—

full_transcript.speaker: Greg Kara
full_transcript.text: you can run like demons are sort of running always in the background and cron jobs run like at a certain time and do something. But the idea that like the machine has a heartbeat and it will do things each time it does this heartbeat is like really thoughtful and interesting and it adds this new layer of kind of being able to your point, like run run recursive things, like have it do things like every five minutes, every 15 minutes. And it it has this alive feel to it that I think um has uh is really really interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: George Yameen
full_transcript.text: It feels like it's looking out for you and wants to help you proactively and that's a new kind of experience for software products.

full_transcript.speaker: Greg Kara
full_transcript.text: Every major advancement we've seen in AI really comes down to better and more refined data. From autonomous vehicles to humanoid robots to LLM chatbots that we use every day, day in and day out. And when models are trained on high-quality, diverse, real-world datasets, well, they get smarter and they get smarter faster and it's more reliable. That's why Uber has introduced AI solutions to help your company build great AI products on top of world-class data. Now, obviously Uber has mastered the art of data collection and labeling. They're using it right now. Millions of trips around the world every hour. And Uber has an international collective of experts that is going to help you source, label, and evaluate real-world high-quality data on the scale that best fits your project, whether that's super broad or hyper niche. You can get on with the real job building next best thing and have Uber as your partner for data. Book your demo today by going to uber.com/twist. That's uber.com/TWIST.

full_transcript.text: Alright now Dave, we're going to get into the Pete Flint transaction and the foundation stuff, but first we have to take a short break to talk about something very important, which is Plaud. If you don't write it down, it doesn't exist, if you don't have a checklist, you won't get it done, and we all move a bit too fast to allow time for forgetfulness. And this is why we love the Plaud note pen, which is currently...

full_transcript.text: All right, I got it from here. Okay. I got it from here. This is the first time Plaud's been on, but I've applauded Plaud before. Dave, you see this thing on the back? It's a- it's a wallet. It looks like a wallet, but inside the wallet is my Plaud. Um, and what it is is a little thin piece of hardware and you'll see there's a light on it and um, there's a little button that you can barely see. When I hold the button down, it starts and stops and it does a little haptic. So I hold it and then it starts recording. When I go out on the ski mountain, I start it recording and I have a Plaud pin I wear, which is a different form factor. And so when I start my day, I record. Now, I'm on this podcast, it's recording my side, it can't hear your side, but I just talk to it all day long. And if I'm in a meeting, I use it and I put it on the table and it has like a little indicator that it's recording and so it's like moving a little strip across. Then at the end of the day, it knows when I say action item, it knows when I say tell this person to do this or please...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Ask this person about that or remind me about stuff. And so I do production meetings now for the podcast. I pop up in my Plaud, I do the production meeting and I say here's what I want to talk about on the pod, these are the four or five topics, and I will be talking to two producers or two executives at the firm. And then I can just summarize it and it has a whole gallery of different transcripts, summaries, action items, all different ones being created like the skills. And then I can send a link immediately to another team member and they just have it. And because it's part of the hardware, I don't have to open my phone, it's so not intrusive.

full_transcript.text: Perfect.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Alright, Alex, you must have a question here. Sorry to monopolize the first part of our discussion.

full_transcript.speaker: Greg Kara
full_transcript.text: Oh no, you're fine. When it comes to friends, I just sit back. But Dave, the foundation was amazing news as Jason said. Everyone was very excited that there was something in place to ensure that Open Cloud would continue. So what I want to know is how early were you guys working on the foundation, who else is involved, and then of course what your goals are for the next three to six months.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: You know, from the first conversation, I reached out to Peter early January out of pure gratitude, just to tell him like thank you for building this, and that started a conversation. And this was before anybody, I mean there were like hundreds of people in the Discord, and you know we now have over 100,000. And we also just passed Linux and React yesterday in GitHub stars, so we're the number one open source project in the world.

full_transcript.speaker: George Yameen
full_transcript.text: That's crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Immediately my impulse was to just help Peter like look, whatever this turns into I want to be part of helping protect it. Like it's too important. I think Jason, like you were saying, as soon as you become Cloud-pilled, you're immediately just like this is so important.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Have to protect it.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And having been around the technology business a very long time and been through several different platforms, even having been part of building, you know, the Facebook platform back in the day, I've seen a lot of what can go right and what can go wrong. And so I just immediately told him like let's get started trying to figure out if there's a foundation here.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: No matter what you decide, you know, he's been very open about his thinking. I mean, you can just go on Lex or any of the podcasts that he's done and he was very open about his thinking and what he was thinking through. And so that was the idea was just like, let's make sure that we have an open source project here that's a real thing that has a real, you know, 501(c)(3) nonprofit foundation behind it. There's a lot of precedent for this, be it, you know, Linux or, you know, any number of the open source foundations, Apache, Mozilla. There's a lot of this stuff out there. And let's do this right and make sure that both the technical side, Peter never loses, you know, decision making authority over and on the open source side that we set this up with good governance. Let's try to get as many of the big labs involved as possible and try to make it the Switzerland of AI because that's really I think the experience that people have once they start using it. They're like, wow, I can use my own Mac as an orchestrator to interact with all the different AIs however I want. And so the foundation should play the same role in the ecosystem. And so that's what we're working towards.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Dave, I will get back to the major labs in a second, but tell me more about Peter maintaining essentially direction or decision making. What is his continuing role and how much authority does he wield still?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Look, it's his project. We're still working out all of the governance. We're like deep in the middle of it right now. But it's Peter's project. And so I think you can expect that he's going to make the decisions, especially the technical ones. And the foundation exists to protect it, not to control it, try to, you know, influence its direction. The foundation has to be in the middle of the broader community like you guys and taking input, making sure that the global conversation is heard. But at the end of the day, Peter built this thing. And so, you know, we're just here to serve.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: On the sponsorship point, you do have OpenAI, Vercel, Blacksmith and recently announced Convex as sponsors of the project. You mentioned getting other AI labs in there. Anthropic famously got a little bit twisty with the project originally over some naming rights and so forth. But do you expect to get Anthropic, xAI, Mistral and maybe even the Gemini team to all also support?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Yeah, let's hope so. You know, we've got conversations going with everyone. We'll figure it out as time plays out. And you know, we're just excited to see where it nets out.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: These open source projects, when they get this big, you get a very large community involved. What becomes the dynamics in terms of just politics?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Explain to the audience, because I think it's confusing for people, who gets to or how are all the different changes—how does air traffic control work for the different changes at a—at scale project like this? Because there's so many people wanting to contribute, obviously skills can be done independent of the—of the—the core codebase, but how does that work, and is that what the foundation needs to do, is like maybe hire air traffic control to—to manage it?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so the way that it works is that anybody in the world can download the project. You know, you can go on GitHub, uh, you can download a copy of it, you can make changes, uh, you know, improve it, and then you could submit pull requests—or as everybody, uh, you know, you're probably on Twitter or if you're not familiar with the nomenclature, people call them PRs. Um, and what that means is that you're submitting a change to the open source project and you'd like to, uh, have it included next time there's a release. Now, when a project gets this big, the number of PRs goes through the roof. Um, I actually haven't looked today, uh, you know, I think we're up above 5,000 at any given time.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Listen, we all know ChatGPT is amazing when you just need to look something up quickly. But, if you're not going really deep and getting strategic when you write your prompts, you're not getting the most out of this incredible tool. But I've got a free resource from our friends at HubSpot. It's called Advanced ChatGPT Prompt Engineering: From Basic to Expert in 7 Days. HubSpot is giving you a complete system for structuring ChatGPT prompts using the ROSES framework: Role, Objective, Scenario, Expected solution, and Steps. Thinking about your prompts this way is a huge game changer, and it's taken me from just hoping I get the right output I'm looking for to knowing with confidence that I'm going to get the information I need, organized the way I want. So, level up the use of your AI and download the guide for free at clickhubspot.com/twist2. That's clickhubspot.com/TWIST and the number 2. Or, find the link in the description. And thanks to HubSpot for sponsoring this episode.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: I mean, it's so many more PRs than are possible to, uh, work through. There is a group of people called the maintainers. Um, the maintainers of an open source project are usually a much smaller crew, you know, I don't know, different projects have different numbers, 20, 30, 40, 50 people, um, that watch all of the PRs coming in. They also have their own ideas for what needs to be improved, and they prioritize what's most important.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: So, the most important thing for the last few weeks has been security. Uh, whenever you have a really large project that a lot of people start working with, uh, security becomes number one. Um, and so there've been a lot of security, uh, updates. It also, you know, in this case, we're working on AI, and so there's a lot of different models being released, uh, every week.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: I think there's a lot of new things happening, trying to include more models, more integrations, stuff that makes sense. A lot of requests to integrate this, integrate that, like, "I want to have this part of my workflow, will you put this in the core project?" right? Like, all those kinds of things. And so the maintainers have to look at all of that and make editorial decisions as to what will improve the project. And we've really been in improve-the-project, you know, from a stability, security, like how it works today. We have not been working on, you know, as much the new features right now.

full_transcript.speaker: George Yameen
full_transcript.text: Do the maintainers get paid by the foundation, or are they just like super volunteers that have extra, you know, merge-like privileges?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Right now they're super volunteers. And we ideally would like to, you know, bring on some of the best, the people that are most interested, those kinds of things. And, you know, once we have the capital in the foundation and these kinds of things, then we ideally will pay them for their work to the extent we can.

full_transcript.speaker: George Yameen
full_transcript.text: So Dave, how do we measure success here? Because we've been watching the GitHub stars go vertical for weeks now, incredibly impressive, I don't mean to diminish that. But for the foundation, what does success look like in stewarding this project? And what's kind of like the North Star, apart from improving the product, handling security issues, and interacting with the community? Like, where do you want it to go?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think that success looks like a stable and growing ecosystem, right? Like, you could perhaps look at how many projects are there out there, both, not, you know, open source that have included OpenCLaw, you could look at commercial projects that are built on top of OpenCLaw. And that we have a really just stable, beautiful, growing ecosystem, not unlike Linux. You know, I think most of the early internet was built on Linux, right? Most people don't know that, but that's the truth. And so, to the extent that this can be a huge part of how people build things going forward, I think that's success for us. I also think we have to continue research, improving things going forward, making it better. OpenCLaw, at the end of the day, was a very powerful idea. You know, the idea that you can give people the power of AI on their own computer, this idea of a personal AI that actually does things, takes actions for you. I think this is a major part of the future, and we have to just continue to push towards that frontier.

full_transcript.speaker: George Yameen
full_transcript.text: Is it possible to donate to the foundation yet? Is there like a donation page yet? And then we'll get to the business side.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: Not yet. Yeah, we're hoping, you know, within the next couple of weeks, as we start to get the business side together.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave Morin
full_transcript.text: You probably know, anybody who's set up all the various types of business entities in the world, 501(c)(3)s are one of the most sophisticated. So, we're working through it as fast as we can.

They've got a new, uh, a new virtual machine environment called Sandboxes, and so I've been doing a bunch of testing using Sandboxes. They're not really set up yet for OpenClaw to be super effective. You have to keep them alive every five hours and these kinds of things. But, um, doing a bunch of testing there. But I think the answer to the question is whatever your use case is, you know.

highlights: Open Claw has emerged as the world’s leading open-source project, surpassing Linux and React in GitHub stars, and is now governed by a non-profit foundation designed to protect it as the "Switzerland of AI.".

The "Claw-pilled" movement emphasizes the shift toward personal AI that runs locally on private hardware, utilizing unique features like "Heartbeats" for proactive task execution and local memory files for data sovereignty.

Investment interest is shifting from simple vertical AI co-pilots toward the "orchestration layer," where a central AI acts as a "maestro" to manage multiple specialized agents for complex business workflows.

There is a significant industry debate over "red lines" in AI development, highlighted by Anthropic’s refusal to support domestic surveillance or autonomous lethal weaponry in government contracts despite the potential for massive revenue.

Emerging agentic tools now allow founders to automate highly technical tasks, such as using AI agents to watch live user sessions and automatically deploy real-time AB tests to optimize app conversion rates.
summary:
This transcript from *This Week in Startups*, set in March 2026, centers on the explosive rise of **Open Claw**, an open-source AI project that has surpassed Linux and React in GitHub popularity. Guest Dave Morin, the first board member of the Open Claw Foundation, discusses the "Claw-pilled" phenomenon—the moment users realize the power of a recursive, proactive personal AI. A major theme is the shift toward local AI orchestration, where users run models on their own hardware (like Mac Studios) to maintain data privacy and prevent "platform risk" or "rug-pulling" from large corporations like OpenAI or Anthropic. The Open Claw Foundation is presented as a "Switzerland of AI," a non-profit designed to protect the project's longevity and neutrality while allowing its creator, Peter, to maintain technical direction.

The discussion also explores the evolving landscape for AI entrepreneurs and the ethical implications of the technology. Morin and Calacanis advise founders to focus on the "orchestration layer" and the creation of unique, off-model datasets to avoid being subsumed by major AI labs. The episode features demos from Run Tools AI, which provides 3D virtual office environments for agents, and Pickle Watch, which uses AI to automate A/B testing and user behavior analysis. These demos highlight the practical shift toward "agentic workflows" that replace traditional middle-management tasks.

Finally, the transcript touches on the intersection of AI and geopolitics, specifically addressing Anthropic’s refusal to meet Department of Defense terms regarding "murder bots" and domestic surveillance. The speakers debate whether this is a moral stance or a savvy marketing move to capture the consumer "face" in the AI wars. Calacanis concludes with his investment thesis on the future of military tech, expressing interest in "non-lethal" autonomous systems and immobilization technologies, while emphasizing the necessity of thoughtful legislation to prevent the abuse of AI-driven domestic surveillance.

Today’s show:
 
Dave Morin joined the show today to explain how the OpenClaw Foundation came into existence, and what its goals are. Fear not, Peter Steinberger retains directional control of the project. But if you want to sustain an open-source project over time, you need a corporate entity, well-heeled sponsors, maintainers and more. Morin is helping make sure that OpenClaw can stay true to itself well into the future.

TWIST also put the day’s most critical AI stories to Morin for his insight: What’s his take on the Anthropic-DoW fracas? Does Morin think that OpenAI and Anthropic are catching up to OpenClaw in agentic terms? And will OpenAI cede the consumer crown to Anthropic? 

Next came two amazing demos: Runtools’ CTO Greg Kara showed off a 3D interface for OpenClaw that his team built, allowing ‘Claw users to watch their agents work in real time, and even read over their shoulder. Then PickelWatch founder George Yameen showed off an agnetic workflow using OpenClaw and Claude’s Chrome plugin side-by-side. The gist? Make sure that the agentic service you select is cost-effective.
